title: Mistaken Identity

jokes.text: Why can't Geoffrey baby-sit her? Because we're giving him the weekend off. You mean I'm going to have to get my own breakfast, too?
jokes.setup: Carlton asks why Geoffrey can't babysit instead
jokes.punchline: Carlton is more concerned about getting his own breakfast than babysitting his sister
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy showing Carlton's privileged, self-centered nature. Well-structured with clear setup/payoff.

jokes.text: Am I being punished for something? I was just asking myself that same question.
jokes.setup: Carlton asks if he's being punished for having to babysit
jokes.punchline: Philip implies Carlton IS the punishment (for having him as a son)
jokes.explanation: Nice dry comeback from Philip. Very economical delivery with good comic timing.

jokes.text: But, Dad, I'm only nine. Can't Hilary take care of herself?
jokes.setup: Ashley protests being too young to babysit
jokes.punchline: She suggests the older sister should take care of herself instead
jokes.explanation: Smart character-driven joke showing Ashley's mature insight into Hilary's helplessness.

jokes.text: Well, I'm in a bad mood, and somebody is going to suffer.
jokes.setup: Hilary enters the scene
jokes.punchline: She immediately announces her intention to make others miserable
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary line - self-absorbed and dramatically threatening. Great character integration.

jokes.text: Yo, Palm Springs, the home of the dental floss bikini.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Palm Springs destination
jokes.punchline: Will's crude but colorful description of the local attraction
jokes.explanation: Classic Will Smith delivery with vivid imagery. Great economy and character voice.

jokes.text: You're not going. You better tell that to my mojo because his bags is packed.
jokes.setup: Philip tells Will he's not going to Palm Springs
jokes.punchline: Will personifies his 'mojo' as having already packed
jokes.explanation: Excellent Will Smith wordplay with creative personification. Very quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: Right away, sir. I shall tend to my hernia a bit later.
jokes.setup: Philip asks Geoffrey to answer the door
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey deadpans about delaying medical attention to serve
jokes.explanation: Perfect Geoffrey dry wit - suffering in service with deadpan delivery. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: Did you see that movie House Party? No. Did you see The Terminator?
jokes.setup: Will references House Party (party movie) as warning
jokes.punchline: Philip counters with The Terminator (violent action movie) as threat
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation joke with perfect movie references. Uncle Phil's unexpected threat is both funny and character-revealing.

jokes.text: Yo, G. Did you leave a vacuum cleaner on? I just heard a loud sucking noise.
jokes.setup: Carlton is aggressively complimenting the Furths
jokes.punchline: Will compares Carlton's sucking up to literal sucking sound
jokes.explanation: Classic Will insult with good visual metaphor. Perfect timing after Carlton's over-the-top flattery.

jokes.text: This is a Black thing, isn't it?
jokes.setup: Carlton realizes he got chosen over Will for the Palm Springs trip
jokes.punchline: Carlton's naive recognition of potential racial dynamics
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment showing Carlton's sheltered perspective. Sets up the episode's racial themes with perfect naivety.

jokes.text: Four: Get the stick out of your butt!
jokes.setup: Carlton lists his boring, studious plans for Palm Springs
jokes.punchline: Will adds his own crude item to Carlton's formal list
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Carlton's formality and Will's bluntness. Crude but character-appropriate.

jokes.text: What do you say to an Oreo? I say, 'What's up, Carlton?'
jokes.setup: Carlton asks what Will would say to an Oreo cookie
jokes.punchline: Will treats it as introduction, comparing Carlton to an Oreo (black outside, white inside)
jokes.explanation: Masterful wordplay that works on multiple levels. Brilliant use of the Oreo metaphor for Carlton's character while subverting the setup perfectly.

jokes.text: Look, thanks a lot, man. Now we got to go back and get my head! And I really liked that head.
jokes.setup: Will hits his head on a low bridge
jokes.punchline: Will dramatically mourns his injured head as if it's been lost
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy with Will's characteristic dramatic overreaction. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Punch it, man. We're in a Benz. Are you crazy? I call this a lucky break. A policeman is our pal. We can ask him for directions.
jokes.setup: Police approach their car
jokes.punchline: Will and Carlton have completely opposite reactions - Will wants to flee, Carlton sees opportunity
jokes.explanation: Perfect character contrast setting up the episode's central conflict. Carlton's naivety vs. Will's street wisdom.

jokes.text: Fool, this is not The Love Connection.
jokes.setup: Carlton is overly friendly and chatty with the police officer
jokes.punchline: Will compares Carlton's behavior to a dating show appearance
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture reference that captures Carlton's inappropriate friendliness with police.

jokes.text: He's going to tell us to get out of the car. You watch too much TV. Get out of the car.
jokes.setup: Will predicts they'll be asked to exit, Carlton dismisses this as TV fiction
jokes.punchline: Officer immediately proves Will right
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic timing. Carlton's dismissal immediately proven wrong enhances the episode's themes about racial profiling.

jokes.text: Where are you from? LA. Not exactly... Next question, please.
jokes.setup: Officer asks where they're from, Will gives short answer
jokes.punchline: Carlton starts to elaborate, Will cuts him off demanding next question
jokes.explanation: Shows Will trying to control the situation while Carlton wants to over-explain

jokes.text: Actually we come from Bel-Air. In fact, we live two houses away from the Reagans. We have the same pool man.
jokes.setup: Carlton tries to establish their respectable background
jokes.punchline: His attempts become increasingly absurd, ending with shared pool maintenance
jokes.explanation: Great escalating absurdity showing Carlton's privileged mindset and desperation to prove respectability.

jokes.text: Sure. And while you're at it... you might want to give Ron and Nancy a ring-a-ding-ding.
jokes.setup: Carlton mentions living near the Reagans
jokes.punchline: Officer sarcastically suggests calling them too
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows the officer's skepticism. The 'ring-a-ding-ding' adds nice comedic flourish.

jokes.text: Yale? Marvelous. I've got to go now. See you soon, dear.
jokes.setup: Carlton says he's in jail
jokes.punchline: Mrs. Furth mishears it as 'Yale' and congratulates him
jokes.explanation: Classic misunderstanding gag that works well with the character types and situation.

jokes.text: It probably wasn't Yale or Carlton. It probably wasn't even the phone.
jokes.setup: Philip questions Margaret's interpretation of the call
jokes.punchline: He escalates to doubting the entire phone call happened
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating doubt that shows Philip's frustration with Margaret while being genuinely funny.

jokes.text: Hilary, you're sitting right next to the phone. I don't care. I'm your babysitter. I command you.
jokes.setup: Phone rings and Ashley asks Hilary to answer it
jokes.punchline: Hilary asserts babysitting authority to avoid basic tasks
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary character moment showing her laziness and twisted logic about authority.

jokes.text: Let me know if it's for me. But, Miss Hilary, I'm off duty. And you're sitting right next to the phone. Yeah, but you're facing the phone.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey reluctantly answers phone, Hilary wants to know if it's for her
jokes.punchline: Hilary's absurd logic that facing direction determines phone-answering duty
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd logic from Hilary. The escalating laziness and geometric reasoning is perfectly character-specific.

jokes.text: Lazy House.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey answers the phone
jokes.punchline: Instead of saying the house name, he describes its current state
jokes.explanation: Masterful Geoffrey moment - perfect economy, character voice, and clever wordplay. One of the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: I'm tired of doing favors for you spoilt children. I'm off duty. I am not an animal.
jokes.setup: Will asks Geoffrey for a favor
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's dramatic declaration of independence, ending with dignity assertion
jokes.explanation: Great Geoffrey rebellion moment with escalating indignation. The 'I am not an animal' declaration is perfectly dramatic.

jokes.text: I have a 3.95 grade point average... which would have been 4.0, except I had to take a ceramics class... and my pot exploded in my kiln.
jokes.setup: Carlton tries to establish his credentials to police
jokes.punchline: His perfect GPA was ruined by a pottery accident
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton character moment - his academic obsession and the absurd specificity of the ceramics failure.

jokes.text: Neither will I. The cops took my fake ID.
jokes.setup: Carlton worries about not being admitted to the bar (law bar)
jokes.punchline: Will thinks he means drinking bar and reveals his fake ID was confiscated
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection playing on double meaning of 'bar' - shows character differences perfectly.

jokes.text: Any time you see a White guy in jail, you know he did something bad.
jokes.setup: Will is nervous about a fellow prisoner
jokes.punchline: Will's dark logic about racial dynamics in the justice system
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversive humor that inverts racial stereotypes while making a serious point. Perfectly crafted and memorable.

jokes.text: Word to Big Bird. We flicked eight Benzitos, 15 Jags, and a Maserati! But I didn't like the upholstery so I took it back, Jack!
jokes.setup: Will gives fake confession on live TV
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top criminal persona, complete with upholstery preferences
jokes.explanation: Great Will performance moment - the fake street slang and absurd criminal pickiness about car interiors.

jokes.text: This is the biggest amateur operation I have seen since Mayberry R.F.D.
jokes.setup: Vivian criticizes the police handling of the situation
jokes.punchline: She compares them to the bumbling fictional police from Andy Griffith Show
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture reference that works for the character and situation, though not exceptional.

jokes.text: Calm yourself, Barney Fife.
jokes.setup: Officer tells Vivian to calm down
jokes.punchline: Vivian compares him to the bumbling deputy from Andy Griffith Show
jokes.explanation: Good callback extending the Mayberry theme. Very economical insult.

jokes.text: Open the cell, Jerry.
jokes.setup: Philip delivers intimidating legal threat
jokes.punchline: Officer's matter-of-fact surrender to superior authority
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective deflation of tension. The casual nature of the surrender works well.

jokes.text: or I'll tie this place up with so much litigation... that your grandchildren are gonna need lawyers!
jokes.setup: Philip threatens legal action
jokes.punchline: Escalates to multi-generational lawsuit threat
jokes.explanation: Philip in full lawyer mode - threat escalates to ridiculous time scale showing his legal prowess

jokes.text: Peace out, Bob.
jokes.setup: Will is leaving jail
jokes.punchline: He casually says goodbye to his cellmate using street vernacular
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Will's adaptability and casual attitude toward the situation.

jokes.text: To reward her, we went to this café that's owned by John Cougar Mellencamp... Kim Basinger, and Doogie Howser's business manager.
jokes.setup: Hilary describes rewarding Ashley
jokes.punchline: She name-drops bizarre celebrity ownership of a café
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary character moment with absurd celebrity name-dropping. The Doogie Howser business manager detail is particularly funny.

jokes.text: Hi, Mom! Hi, Dad! Let's go play! Come on, everybody, let's go play!
jokes.setup: Ashley had espresso
jokes.punchline: She's hyperactively bouncing around from caffeine
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy payoff to the espresso setup. Predictable but functional.

jokes.text: Arrested? By who? The fashion police?
jokes.setup: Family mentions being arrested
jokes.punchline: Hilary's only frame of reference for arrest is fashion-related
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary character moment - her shallow worldview means she can only imagine fashion-related arrests.

jokes.text: We were breaking the slowness limit. Okay, I've never heard of that law before. But I did hear this other law. It's the 'if you see a Black guy driving anything but a burned-out Pinto... you better stop him because he stole it' law.
jokes.setup: Will sarcastically accepts Carlton's 'slowness limit' explanation
jokes.punchline: Will explains the real 'law' - racial profiling with brutal honesty
jokes.explanation: Brilliant, hard-hitting social commentary disguised as comedy. The 'burned-out Pinto' detail is perfect specificity. One of the episode's strongest moments.

jokes.text: We were breaking the slowness limit. Okay, I've never heard of that law before. But I did hear this other law. It's the 'if you see a Black guy driving anything but a burned-out Pinto... you better stop him because he stole it' law.
jokes.setup: Carlton insists they were stopped for driving slowly
jokes.punchline: Will sarcastically describes racial profiling as 'law'
jokes.explanation: Powerful social commentary through bitter humor - 'burned-out Pinto' detail makes it visceral and specific

jokes.text: No map is going to save you, and neither is your glee club... or your fancy Bel-Air address or who your daddy is. When you're driving in a nice car in a strange neighborhood... none of that matters. They only see one thing.
jokes.setup: Carlton continues to blame the map
jokes.punchline: Will delivers harsh reality about racial profiling
jokes.explanation: Powerful dramatic moment that transcends comedy. Will's frustration and the episode's central theme delivered with perfect character integration. This is what made Fresh Prince special.

jokes.text: I asked myself that question the first time I was stopped. Good night, son.
jokes.setup: Carlton asks if Philip would stop a slow car
jokes.punchline: Philip reveals he's also experienced racial profiling
jokes.explanation: Devastating final moment that recontextualizes Philip's character. Even success and wealth don't protect from racial profiling. Perfect ending note.

tmdb_overview: Jailed, Will and Carlton stage a media stunt.
